speaker: seller

args.message: Ah, okay—look, that dent is very minor. It's a cosmetic ding, honestly. I didn't mention it upfront because it's not collision damage in the sense of a recorded accident—this was a parking lot bump, super common stuff. Could've happened anytime in the car's life. The previous owner got it touched up, which is why it blends in pretty well. It doesn't affect the structural integrity or the function of the car at all. In the used car market, a 3-inch dent like that is pretty standard wear and tear on a 2018. Doesn't show up on Carfax because it was never reported to insurance—just a minor ding somebody fixed themselves. I should've mentioned it, fair point, but it's truly cosmetic and honestly doesn't impact the value meaningfully. It's the kind of thing you see on every used car on the lot.

args.message: Title came back clean—no salvage, no liens, that's good. But let me be direct: you said upfront this car had "zero collision damage" and was "meticulously maintained" and in "top condition," then when pressed on specifics, you couldn't provide documentation on brake pads, tire tread, or battery dates. I'm asking because I'm still interested in this car, but I need straight answers before we talk price.

Last documented service was at 68,500 miles, and we're at 78,000 now, so it's about 4,200 miles past due. That's the main thing. I should've flagged that earlier instead of saying it was done recently—that was wrong of me. The dent, the overdue oil—those are the two things that aren't ideal. Everything else checks out mechanically based on what I know. I'm not trying to hide anything else. Those are the two items that matter. I get why you want to verify everything yourself, and frankly, you should. But I'm being straight with you now: that's what we're working with. What would it take to move forward?

text: Interior inspection too—good call covering everything. What did that turn up?

speaker: buyer

args.message:
Interior came back clean. Here's where I'm at: You've disclosed two material issues—an undisclosed quarter-panel dent and a 4,200-mile overdue oil change. You also initially misrepresented the maintenance history. Those are real issues that reduce the car's value and condition below what you advertised. 

At $18,495, I'm paying premium price for a car that needs service and has cosmetic damage. The engine and transmission are solid, title is clean, interior is good—that's the positives. But the deferred maintenance and the dishonesty on disclosure are problems.

I'm offering $16,200. That accounts for the oil change ($150 service), the cosmetic repair ($400–500 to do it right), and a $1,700–1,800 adjustment for the misleading upfront pitch and trust issue. It's still a fair price for a mechanically sound Camry, but it reflects actual condition, not marketing copy.

What do you think?
